questions.question: A problem L is NP-complete if it satisfies which two conditions?
options: L is in P and L is NP-hard
L is in NP and every problem in NP reduces to L in polynomial time
L is decidable and L is NP-hard
L is in EXP and L reduces to SAT
questions.explanation: NP-complete requires: (1) L ∈ NP — yes-instances have poly-time verifiable witnesses, and (2) L is NP-hard — every problem in NP poly-time reduces to L. Being in P alone is insufficient; being decidable alone is insufficient.

questions.question: What does A ≤_p B (A polynomial-time reduces to B) mean?
options: A is easier than B
There is a poly-time function f where x ∈ A ⟺ f(x) ∈ B
A and B have the same time complexity
B can solve A in logarithmic time
questions.explanation: A ≤_p B means there exists a polynomial-time computable function f that maps instances of A to instances of B, preserving membership. If you can solve B fast, you can solve A fast by transforming the input and calling B's solver.

questions.question: The Cook-Levin theorem proves NP-completeness of which problem, and by what technique?
options: 3-SAT, via graph reduction
SAT, via tableau construction encoding NTM computations as Boolean formulas
Vertex Cover, via gadget construction
Halting Problem, via diagonalization
questions.explanation: Cook-Levin proves SAT is NP-complete by taking any NP problem's NTM and constructing a Boolean formula whose satisfying assignments correspond to accepting tableau rows. The formula has cell, transition, start, and accept constraints — all polynomial-size.

questions.question: If you reduce SAT to 3-SAT and then to Vertex Cover, what can you conclude about Vertex Cover?
options: Vertex Cover is in P
Vertex Cover is NP-complete
Vertex Cover is undecidable
Vertex Cover is in EXP but not NP
questions.explanation: The chain SAT ≤_p 3-SAT ≤_p Vertex Cover proves Vertex Cover is NP-hard. Since Vertex Cover is clearly in NP (a subset of k vertices is a verifiable witness), it is NP-complete. This is exactly how the NP-completeness landscape is built: one seed problem (SAT) and chains of reductions.

questions.question: You discover your scheduling problem is NP-complete. What is the most practical engineering response?
options: Give up — the problem is unsolvable
Use a SAT/SMT solver, an approximation algorithm, or heuristic search
Wait for someone to prove P = NP
Only solve instances with fewer than 3 variables
questions.explanation: NP-completeness means no known polynomial algorithm exists, but the problem is still decidable. Practical responses: encode as SAT/Z3, use approximation algorithms (Vertex Cover has a 2-approx), use parameterized algorithms (FPT), or apply heuristics like simulated annealing.
